topic: FFI - OsString and OsStr for Platform-Native Paths

turns.content: Hey, I'm just getting started with Rust at my new job. We use FFI a lot here because of some legacy C code. I've read about OsString and OsStr but still feel confused—can you explain how they relate to platform-native paths?

Sure! Think of it like this: Imagine you're talking to your friend from another country, and sometimes you use English (UTF-8) and other times you use a special code (like UTF-16 in Windows). OsStr is like the special code—a byte slice that Rust understands on any platform. OsString is like the original message you'd write in English—it’s owned by Rust but can be converted to OsStr when needed.
So, if I call a C library expecting a UTF-8 string and pass an OsString, will it always work?

Not exactly. On Windows, OsString is internally represented as WTF-8 (UTF-16 with null terminators). So if you convert it directly to utf-8 bytes via into_string(), it might fail or produce garbage on POSIX systems. That's why it’s safer to use as_encoded_bytes() which gives you the raw encoded bytes that are platform-native—whether UTF-8, UTF-16, or whatever.
